title: About
text:
This project combines historical and real-time financial data with AI to produce automated research reports for US growth stock investors. By feeding macroeconomic indicators, equity market data, and commodity prices into an AI model, each report identifies historical patterns, draws parallels to current conditions, and highlights risks and opportunities for growth-oriented portfolios.
All charts and visualizations in the reports are created by the author — the AI generates only the written analysis.

title: About
text:
Data Sources
Every report is grounded in real data from:

FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data): Inflation (CPI), GDP growth, unemployment, Fed balance sheet, Treasury yields, household debt, recession indicators, Fed funds rate, and high-yield credit spreads
Yahoo Finance: MSCI World Index, S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, Dow Jones, VIX, gold, and Brent crude oil
Multpl.com: S&P 500 trailing P/E ratios

title: About
text:
Disclaimer
This site is for informational and educational purposes only. Nothing here constitutes financial advice. The AI-generated analysis may contain errors, hallucinations, or outdated information. Always do your own research and consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.

title: Market Research
text:
Title: Growth at a Crossroads: Navigating Elevated Valuations in a Late-Cycle U.S. Market
Global markets over the last quarter tell a story of diverging fortunes. While the IMF’s January 2026 outlook projects steady global growth of 3.3%, equity performance has fractured. The MSCI World Index declined 1.4% year-to-date through March, masking stark regional differences: U.S. indices like the S&P 500 (flat, +0.4% YTD) and Nasdaq-100 (-0.6% YTD) have stalled, while ex-U.S. markets showed earlier strength. This divergence, coupled with rising long-term yields—the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield climbed 13 basis points in March alone—echoes the “U.S. exceptionalism” narrative that often precedes a global rebalancing. The resilience of the U.S. economy, however, continues to anchor global sentiment.
For a U.S. growth investor, the current landscape is rich with historical parallels that demand careful scrutiny. The market’s internals point to a late-cycle environment characterized by high valuations, persistent inflation pressures, and a Federal Reserve navigating a delicate path. The S&P 500’s trailing P/E ratio, which ended March 2026 at 27.97, is trading at levels that have historically preceded periods of consolidation or drawdown. More pointedly, the Nasdaq-100’s P/E, despite its recent pullback, remains elevated, reminiscent of the speculative excess seen in the late 1990s, though today’s companies generally have stronger cash flows and clearer paths to profitability. The macro backdrop strengthens this parallel: the Fed has been executing quantitative tightening for over a year, steadily shrinking its balance sheet from a peak of nearly $9 trillion to $6.65 trillion as of March 2026, a trajectory similar to the 2018-2019 cycle that contributed to significant volatility.



MSCI World Equity Index


A key similarity to past boom/bust periods is the configuration of interest rates and credit conditions. The 2-year/10-year Treasury yield spread, while positive in March, was negative for most of 2022 and 2023—a classic recessionary signal. Currently, the 10-year yield at 4.24% and the Fed funds rate at 5.5% (after the December 2025 cut) create a restrictive environment that pressures long-duration assets like growth stocks. This setup is analogous to 2000 and 2007, where a flat or inverted curve, coupled with rising short-term rates, eventually exposed overleveraged sectors. Furthermore, the BofA High-Yield Option-Adjusted Spread, at 3.46 in March 2026, remains relatively contained but has begun to tick up from its 2024 lows, suggesting credit stress is not yet systemic but warrants monitoring, much like in mid-2007.



Performance of Major US Stock Indices


The current environment also presents distinct opportunities. First, the sectoral performance within growth remains bifurcated, creating alpha potential. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) surged 11.7% QTD as of March 5, 2026, driven by insatiable AI demand, a theme with long secular runways. This contrasts sharply with more speculative, cash-flow-negative tech segments that have derated aggressively. Second, household balance sheets, with debt service payments at 11.26% of disposable income as of Q4 2025, remain historically healthy compared to the 13%+ levels seen ahead of the 2008 crisis, providing a buffer for consumer-facing tech.



S&P 500 PE Ratio


However, risks are equally pronounced. The primary risk is an earnings recession. With the S&P 500 trading near 28x trailing earnings, the market is priced for perfection. Any slowdown in the impressive earnings growth that fueled the 2023-2024 rally could lead to a severe derating, similar to the 2001-2002 period. Second, volatility remains suppressed but is showing signs of awakening. The VIX ended March at 24.65, well above its 5-year average of 19.2, indicating increased demand for protection and mirroring the gradual volatility expansion seen in late 2007 and late 2019 before major market shifts.



Cboe Volatility Index (VIX)


In conclusion, the U.S. growth stock market is in a precarious but not hopeless position. It is supported by powerful secular trends (AI, cloud, electrification) and a resilient consumer, yet is burdened by high valuations and a tightening monetary regime that historically compresses multiples. The patterns resemble the late-1990s in terms of valuation and technological fervor, but with the added complication of post-pandemic inflation and a more advanced Fed tightening cycle.
Portfolio Recommendation: 50-65% Exposure to U.S. Growth Stocks.
This reflects a stance of cautious engagement. The secular growth drivers are intact, and pulling back entirely would mean missing potential upside from transformative trends. However, the high valuation backdrop and late-cycle macro signals justify a meaningful allocation to cash or short-term bonds (25-35%) to hedge against volatility and provide dry powder for opportunities. The remaining 10-15% can be opportunistically deployed into non-U.S. markets or alternative assets showing relative value. Focus on quality—companies with strong balance sheets, durable competitive advantages, and clear profitability—and prioritize sectors like semiconductors and enterprise software where earnings momentum remains robust.
